packs.name: 100 New Hanzi
packs.whatsNew: 100 fresh HSK 3-4 characters not already in the app — browse them as their own category in Learn.

packs.name: New Stories · Vol. 1
packs.whatsNew: Story cards now have beautiful artwork.

sentences.pinyin: Jīntiān tiānqì hěn hǎo.
sentences.english: The weather is very nice today.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ hé péngyou qù gōngyuán.
sentences.english: My friend and I go to the park.

sentences.pinyin: Gōngyuán lǐ yǒu hěnduō huā.
sentences.english: There are many flowers in the park.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒmen zuò zài shù xià hē chá.
sentences.english: We sit under a tree and drink tea.

sentences.pinyin: Xiàwǔ wǒmen hěn gāoxìng.
sentences.english: In the afternoon we are very happy.

questions.question: What is the weather like today?
Where do they go?
What do they drink under the tree?

sentences.pinyin: Zhège zhōumò wǒ xiǎng qù lǚyóu.
sentences.english: This weekend I want to travel.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ dǎsuàn zuò huǒchē qù Běijīng.
sentences.english: I plan to take the train to Beijing.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ de péngyou yě yào yìqǐ qù.
sentences.english: My friend also wants to go together.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒmen yào cānguān Chángchéng.
sentences.english: We want to visit the Great Wall.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ xīwàng nèitiān bùyào xiàyǔ.
sentences.english: I hope it won't rain that day.

sentences.chinese: 这一定是一次有意思的旅行。
sentences.pinyin: Zhè yídìng shì yí cì yǒuyìsi de lǚxíng.
sentences.english: This will surely be an interesting trip.

questions.question: Where does the narrator plan to go?
How will they travel?
What do they want to visit?

sentences.chinese: 上个星期，我差点儿错过了火车。
sentences.pinyin: Shàng ge xīngqī, wǒ chàdiǎnr cuòguò le huǒchē.
sentences.english: Last week, I almost missed the train.

sentences.chinese: 那天早上我起床起得太晚了。
sentences.pinyin: Nèitiān zǎoshang wǒ qǐchuáng qǐ de tài wǎn le.
sentences.english: That morning I got up too late.

sentences.chinese: 我马上叫了一辆出租车去火车站。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ mǎshàng jiào le yí liàng chūzūchē qù huǒchēzhàn.
sentences.english: I immediately called a taxi to the train station.

sentences.pinyin: Lùshang hěn dǔ, wǒ fēicháng zháojí.
sentences.english: The roads were jammed, and I was very anxious.

sentences.chinese: 还好，我在火车开走以前到了。
sentences.pinyin: Háihǎo, wǒ zài huǒchē kāizǒu yǐqián dào le.
sentences.english: Luckily, I arrived before the train left.

sentences.chinese: 从那以后，我每次都提前出门。
sentences.pinyin: Cóng nà yǐhòu, wǒ měi cì dōu tíqián chūmén.
sentences.english: Ever since then, I always leave early.

questions.question: Why did the narrator almost miss the train?
How did they get to the station?
What does the narrator do now?

packs.name: New Stories · Vol. 2
packs.whatsNew: Story cards now have beautiful artwork.

sentences.pinyin: Yì nián yǒu sì gè jìjié.
sentences.english: A year has four seasons.

sentences.pinyin: Chūntiān hěn nuǎnhuo, huā dōu kāi le.
sentences.english: Spring is warm, and the flowers bloom.

sentences.pinyin: Xiàtiān hěn rè, wǒ xǐhuan yóuyǒng.
sentences.english: Summer is hot; I like to swim.

sentences.chinese: 秋天天气很好，树叶变黄了。
sentences.pinyin: Qiūtiān tiānqì hěn hǎo, shùyè biàn huáng le.
sentences.english: Autumn weather is nice; the leaves turn yellow.

sentences.pinyin: Dōngtiān hěn lěng, yǒushíhou xiàxuě.
sentences.english: Winter is cold; sometimes it snows.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ zuì xǐhuan chūntiān.
sentences.english: I like spring the most.

questions.question: How many seasons are in a year?
What does the narrator like to do in summer?
Which season does the narrator like most?

sentences.pinyin: Jīntiān zǎoshang kāishǐ xiàyǔ.
sentences.english: It started raining this morning.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ wàng le dài sǎn.
sentences.english: I forgot to bring an umbrella.

sentences.pinyin: Suǒyǐ wǒ de yīfu shī le.
sentences.english: So my clothes got wet.

sentences.pinyin: Xiàwǔ yǔ tíng le, tiān qíng le.
sentences.english: In the afternoon the rain stopped and it cleared up.

sentences.pinyin: Tiānkōng chūxiàn le cǎihóng.
sentences.english: A rainbow appeared in the sky.

sentences.pinyin: Zhēn shì yòu měi yòu tèbié.
sentences.english: It was both beautiful and special.

questions.question: What did the narrator forget to bring?
What happened in the afternoon?
What appeared in the sky?

sentences.chinese: 春节是中国最重要的节日。
sentences.pinyin: Chūnjié shì Zhōngguó zuì zhòngyào de jiérì.
sentences.english: Spring Festival is China's most important holiday.

sentences.chinese: 家人会从各地回家团聚。
sentences.pinyin: Jiārén huì cóng gèdì huíjiā tuánjù.
sentences.english: Family members return home from all over to reunite.

sentences.chinese: 我们一起吃年夜饭，包饺子。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒmen yìqǐ chī niányèfàn, bāo jiǎozi.
sentences.english: We eat the New Year's Eve dinner together and make dumplings.

sentences.pinyin: Háizimen huì shōudào hóngbāo.
sentences.english: The children receive red envelopes.

sentences.chinese: 晚上我们放烟花，看春晚。
sentences.pinyin: Wǎnshang wǒmen fàng yānhuā, kàn Chūnwǎn.
sentences.english: At night we set off fireworks and watch the Spring Festival Gala.

sentences.chinese: 这是一年中最热闹的时候。
sentences.pinyin: Zhè shì yì nián zhōng zuì rènào de shíhou.
sentences.english: This is the liveliest time of the year.

questions.question: What is China's most important holiday?
What do the children receive?
What do they do at night?

sentences.chinese: 我的学校很大也很漂亮。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ de xuéxiào hěn dà yě hěn piàoliang.
sentences.english: My school is big and beautiful.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ měitiān bā diǎn qù shàngkè.
sentences.english: I go to class at eight every day.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ zuì xǐhuan zhōngwén kè.
sentences.english: I like Chinese class the most.

sentences.chinese: 我的老师很好，同学很友好。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ de lǎoshī hěn hǎo, tóngxué hěn yǒuhǎo.
sentences.english: My teacher is nice and my classmates are friendly.

sentences.pinyin: Xiàkè yǐhòu wǒmen yìqǐ wán.
sentences.english: After class we play together.

questions.question: What time does the narrator go to class?
What is the narrator's favourite class?
What do they do after class?

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ zài yì jiā gōngsī gōngzuò.
sentences.english: I work at a company.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ měitiān zuò dìtiě shàngbān.
sentences.english: I take the subway to work every day.

sentences.chinese: 我的工作很忙，但是很有意思。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ de gōngzuò hěn máng, dànshì hěn yǒuyìsi.
sentences.english: My job is busy, but it is interesting.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ jīngcháng hé tóngshì kāihuì.
sentences.english: I often have meetings with colleagues.

sentences.pinyin: Yǒushíhou wǒ děi jiābān.
sentences.english: Sometimes I have to work overtime.

sentences.chinese: 不过我很喜欢我的工作。
sentences.pinyin: Búguò wǒ hěn xǐhuan wǒ de gōngzuò.
sentences.english: Still, I really like my job.

questions.question: How does the narrator get to work?
Who does the narrator have meetings with?
How does the narrator feel about the job?

packs.whatsNew: Story cards now have beautiful artwork.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ yǒu hěn duō àihào.
sentences.english: I have many hobbies.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ xǐhuan tīng yīnyuè hé kàn shū.
sentences.english: I like listening to music and reading.

sentences.pinyin: Zhōumò wǒ chángcháng qù pǎobù.
sentences.english: On weekends I often go running.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ yě xǐhuan huà huà.
sentences.english: I also like drawing.

sentences.pinyin: Hé péngyou yìqǐ wán zuì kuàilè.
sentences.english: Playing with friends is the most fun.

questions.question: What does the narrator like to do?
When does the narrator go running?
What is the most fun?

sentences.pinyin: Jīntiān wǎnshang wǒ zìjǐ zuò fàn.
sentences.english: Tonight I am cooking by myself.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ xiān qù chāoshì mǎi cài.
sentences.english: First I go to the supermarket to buy groceries.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ mǎi le jīdàn, shūcài hé mǐ.
sentences.english: I bought eggs, vegetables and rice.

sentences.pinyin: Zuò cài de shíhou yào xiǎoxīn huǒ.
sentences.english: When cooking, you must be careful with the fire.

sentences.chinese: 我做的菜很好吃，我很高兴。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ zuò de cài hěn hǎochī, wǒ hěn gāoxìng.
sentences.english: The food I made was delicious, and I was very happy.

questions.question: What does the narrator do first?
What did the narrator buy?
How was the food?

sentences.pinyin: Zuótiān wǒ yǒudiǎn bù shūfu.
sentences.english: Yesterday I felt a little unwell.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ tóu téng, yě fāshāo le.
sentences.english: I had a headache, and a fever too.

sentences.pinyin: Suǒyǐ wǒ qù yīyuàn kàn yīshēng.
sentences.english: So I went to the hospital to see a doctor.

sentences.chinese: 医生让我多喝水，多休息。
sentences.pinyin: Yīshēng ràng wǒ duō hē shuǐ, duō xiūxi.
sentences.english: The doctor told me to drink more water and rest more.

sentences.pinyin: Chī le yào yǐhòu, wǒ hǎo duō le.
sentences.english: After taking the medicine, I felt much better.

questions.question: How did the narrator feel yesterday?
options: Unwell, with a headache and fever

questions.question: Where did the narrator go?
What did the doctor advise?

sentences.chinese: 我的手机是我最重要的东西。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ de shǒujī shì wǒ zuì zhòngyào de dōngxi.
sentences.english: My phone is my most important thing.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ yòng shǒujī hé péngyou liáotiān.
sentences.english: I use my phone to chat with friends.

sentences.chinese: 我也用它付钱和看视频。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ yě yòng tā fù qián hé kàn shìpín.
sentences.english: I also use it to pay and watch videos.

sentences.pinyin: Yǒushíhou wǒ yòng de tài duō le.
sentences.english: Sometimes I use it too much.

sentences.chinese: 我应该少看手机，多看书。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ yīnggāi shǎo kàn shǒujī, duō kàn shū.
sentences.english: I should look at my phone less and read more.

questions.question: What does the narrator use the phone for?
What is the narrator's problem?
What does the narrator think they should do?
options: Use the phone less and read more

sentences.chinese: 上个月我第一次坐飞机出国。
sentences.pinyin: Shàng ge yuè wǒ dì-yī cì zuò fēijī chūguó.
sentences.english: Last month I flew abroad for the first time.

sentences.chinese: 我提前两个小时到机场。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ tíqián liǎng ge xiǎoshí dào jīchǎng.
sentences.english: I arrived at the airport two hours early.

sentences.chinese: 我办理了登机手续，然后过安检。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ bànlǐ le dēngjī shǒuxù, ránhòu guò ānjiǎn.
sentences.english: I checked in, then went through security.

sentences.pinyin: Fēijī shàng de fúwù hěn hǎo.
sentences.english: The service on the plane was very good.

sentences.chinese: 虽然有点紧张，但是很顺利。
sentences.pinyin: Suīrán yǒudiǎn jǐnzhāng, dànshì hěn shùnlì.
sentences.english: Although a little nervous, it went smoothly.

sentences.pinyin: Zhè shì yí cì nánwàng de lǚxíng.
sentences.english: It was an unforgettable trip.

questions.question: Why did the narrator go to the airport?
options: To fly abroad for the first time

questions.question: How early did the narrator arrive?
How did the trip go?

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ jiā yǒu yì zhī xiǎo māo.
sentences.english: My family has a little cat.

sentences.pinyin: Tā de míngzi jiào Mīmī.
sentences.english: Its name is Mimi.

sentences.pinyin: Mīmī hěn kě'ài, yě hěn ài wán.
sentences.english: Mimi is very cute and loves to play.

sentences.pinyin: Měitiān zǎoshang tā jiào wǒ qǐchuáng.
sentences.english: Every morning it wakes me up.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ hěn xǐhuan wǒ de xiǎo māo.
sentences.english: I really like my cat.

questions.question: What pet does the narrator have?
What is the pet's name?
What does the pet do every morning?

sentences.chinese: 我想去火车站，但是不认识路。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng qù huǒchēzhàn, dànshì bú rènshi lù.
sentences.english: I want to go to the train station, but I don't know the way.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ wèn yí wèi xiānsheng.
sentences.english: I asked a gentleman.

sentences.pinyin: Tā shuō: yìzhí wǎng qián zǒu.
sentences.english: He said: go straight ahead.

sentences.pinyin: Dào hónglǜdēng wǎng zuǒ guǎi.
sentences.english: At the traffic light, turn left.

sentences.pinyin: Huǒchēzhàn jiù zài nǐ de yòubian.
sentences.english: The train station is right on your right.

sentences.pinyin: Xièxie nín, wǒ zhǎodào le.
sentences.english: Thank you, I found it.

questions.question: Where does the narrator want to go?
Which way at the traffic light?
Did the narrator find the place?

sentences.pinyin: Jīntiān wǒ qù yínháng bànshì.
sentences.english: Today I went to the bank to take care of something.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng huàn yìxiē qián.
sentences.english: I wanted to exchange some money.

sentences.chinese: 银行里人很多，我要排队。
sentences.pinyin: Yínháng lǐ rén hěn duō, wǒ yào páiduì.
sentences.english: There were many people in the bank, so I had to queue.

sentences.chinese: 工作人员很热情，帮我办好了。
sentences.pinyin: Gōngzuò rényuán hěn rèqíng, bāng wǒ bàn hǎo le.
sentences.english: The staff were warm and helped me finish.

sentences.pinyin: Bàn wán yǐhòu, wǒ jiù huíjiā le.
sentences.english: After finishing, I went home.

questions.question: Where did the narrator go?
What did the narrator want to do?
Why did the narrator have to wait?

sentences.chinese: 中秋节是中国的传统节日。
sentences.pinyin: Zhōngqiūjié shì Zhōngguó de chuántǒng jiérì.
sentences.english: The Mid-Autumn Festival is a traditional Chinese holiday.

sentences.chinese: 这一天，家人会一起赏月。
sentences.pinyin: Zhè yì tiān, jiārén huì yìqǐ shǎng yuè.
sentences.english: On this day, family members admire the moon together.

sentences.chinese: 我们一边吃月饼，一边聊天。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒmen yìbiān chī yuèbǐng, yìbiān liáotiān.
sentences.english: We eat mooncakes while chatting.

sentences.pinyin: Yuèliang yòu yuán yòu liàng.
sentences.english: The moon is round and bright.

sentences.pinyin: Zhège jiérì ràng wǒmen tuányuán.
sentences.english: This holiday brings us together.

questions.question: What kind of holiday is the Mid-Autumn Festival?
What do they eat?
How is the moon described?

sentences.chinese: 上周我去一家公司面试。
sentences.pinyin: Shàng zhōu wǒ qù yì jiā gōngsī miànshì.
sentences.english: Last week I went to a company for an interview.

sentences.chinese: 我穿得很正式，也提前到了。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ chuān de hěn zhèngshì, yě tíqián dào le.
sentences.english: I dressed formally and arrived early.

sentences.pinyin: Jīnglǐ wèn le wǒ hěn duō wèntí.
sentences.english: The manager asked me many questions.

sentences.chinese: 我虽然有点紧张，但是回答得很好。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ suīrán yǒudiǎn jǐnzhāng, dànshì huídá de hěn hǎo.
sentences.english: Although I was a bit nervous, I answered well.

sentences.chinese: 几天以后，公司给了我工作。
sentences.pinyin: Jǐ tiān yǐhòu, gōngsī gěi le wǒ gōngzuò.
sentences.english: A few days later, the company gave me the job.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ fēicháng gāoxìng.
sentences.english: I was extremely happy.

questions.question: Why did the narrator go to the company?
How did the narrator prepare?
What was the result?

packs.name: New Stories · Vol. 5
packs.whatsNew: Five new graded-reader stories — buying clothes, the post office, the cinema, the barber's and renting your first flat.

sentences.pinyin: Jīntiān wǒ qù shāngdiàn mǎi yīfu.
sentences.english: Today I'm going to the store to buy clothes.

sentences.chinese: 我想买一件红色的衣服。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng mǎi yí jiàn hóngsè de yīfu.
sentences.english: I want to buy a red piece of clothing.

sentences.pinyin: Zhè jiàn yīfu duōshao qián?
sentences.english: How much is this piece of clothing?

sentences.pinyin: Shòuhuòyuán shuō yìbǎi kuài.
sentences.english: The salesperson says one hundred yuan.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ hěn xǐhuan, jiù mǎi le tā.
sentences.english: I like it a lot, so I bought it.

questions.question: Why did the narrator go to the store?
What colour clothing did the narrator want?
How much did the clothing cost?

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ yào qù yóujú jì yì fēng xìn.
sentences.english: I'm going to the post office to mail a letter.

sentences.pinyin: Zhè fēng xìn yào jì dào Běijīng.
sentences.english: This letter needs to be sent to Beijing.

sentences.chinese: 工作人员问我要不要快递。
sentences.pinyin: Gōngzuò rényuán wèn wǒ yào bu yào kuàidì.
sentences.english: The clerk asks whether I want express delivery.

sentences.chinese: 我说不用，普通的就可以。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ shuō búyòng, pǔtōng de jiù kěyǐ.
sentences.english: I say no need, ordinary mail will do.

sentences.pinyin: Jì xìn huā le wǒ wǔ kuài qián.
sentences.english: Mailing the letter cost me five yuan.

questions.question: What does the narrator want to do at the post office?
Where is the letter being sent?
Did the narrator choose express delivery?

sentences.chinese: 周末我和朋友一起看电影。
sentences.pinyin: Zhōumò wǒ hé péngyou yìqǐ kàn diànyǐng.
sentences.english: On the weekend my friend and I watch a movie together.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒmen mǎi le liǎng zhāng diànyǐng piào.
sentences.english: We bought two movie tickets.

sentences.pinyin: Zhè bù diànyǐng hěn yǒu yìsi.
sentences.english: This movie is very interesting.

sentences.chinese: 我们一边看一边吃爆米花。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒmen yìbiān kàn yìbiān chī bàomǐhuā.
sentences.english: We eat popcorn while watching.

sentences.chinese: 看完电影，我们去吃晚饭。
sentences.pinyin: Kàn wán diànyǐng, wǒmen qù chī wǎnfàn.
sentences.english: After the movie, we go to have dinner.

questions.question: Who did the narrator watch the movie with?
How many tickets did they buy?
What did they do after the movie?

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ de tóufa tài cháng le.
sentences.english: My hair is too long.

sentences.pinyin: Jīntiān wǒ qù lǐfà.
sentences.english: Today I'm going to get a haircut.

sentences.chinese: 理发师问我想要什么样子。
sentences.pinyin: Lǐfàshī wèn wǒ xiǎng yào shénme yàngzi.
sentences.english: The barber asks what style I want.

sentences.pinyin: Wǒ shuō duǎn yìdiǎnr jiù hǎo.
sentences.english: I say a little shorter is fine.

sentences.pinyin: Lǐ wán fà, wǒ juéde hěn jīngshen.
sentences.english: After the haircut, I feel very sharp.

questions.question: Why did the narrator get a haircut?
What style did the narrator ask for?
How did the narrator feel afterwards?

sentences.chinese: 大学毕业以后，我想自己租房子。
sentences.pinyin: Dàxué bìyè yǐhòu, wǒ xiǎng zìjǐ zū fángzi.
sentences.english: After graduating from university, I want to rent a flat on my own.

sentences.chinese: 我在网上找到了一个合适的地方。
sentences.pinyin: Wǒ zài wǎngshàng zhǎodào le yí ge héshì de dìfang.
sentences.english: I found a suitable place online.

sentences.chinese: 这个房子离地铁站很近。
sentences.pinyin: Zhège fángzi lí dìtiězhàn hěn jìn.

packs.name: Inventions & Innovations
packs.whatsNew: Six more Chinese inventions for the History ▸ Inventions tab — the abacus, silk, porcelain, the seismograph, paper money and the kite, each with its own photo and detail page.

sections.body: The Chinese abacus (算盘) is a bead-frame calculating tool refined over the Han and later dynasties. Beads slide on rods divided by a beam — two beads above, five below — letting a skilled user add, subtract, multiply and divide at remarkable speed.

list: Powered commerce, tax offices and banks for centuries
Faster than written arithmetic in trained hands
Still taught today to build number sense

sections.body: Sericulture — raising silkworms and unwinding their cocoons into thread — was a closely guarded Chinese secret for thousands of years. Exporting silkworm eggs was punishable by death.
So prized abroad that the trade routes linking China to Rome were named the Silk Road after it. Silk was used as currency, tribute and diplomatic gift.
Fired at very high temperatures from kaolin clay, true porcelain was so identified with its homeland that in English the material is simply called “china.”
Jingdezhen (景德镇) became the porcelain capital. Ming blue-and-white ware was traded across the world and remains among the most collected ceramics in history.
The polymath Zhang Heng (张衡) built the world's first seismograph in 132 CE — a bronze urn ringed with eight dragons, each holding a ball above an open-mouthed toad.

list: A tremor tipped a pendulum inside the urn
The dragon facing the quake dropped its ball into a toad
The direction of the distant earthquake could be read at once

sections.body: During the Song dynasty, merchants in Sichuan began issuing paper receipts called jiāozǐ (交子) — the world's first paper money — to avoid hauling heavy strings of bronze coins.
The government soon took over issuance, centuries before paper currency appeared in Europe. Marco Polo wrote home in astonishment about money made of mulberry bark.
Invented over 2,000 years ago from light bamboo frames and silk, kites began as a pastime but were quickly put to practical use.

list: Measuring distances and wind
Signalling across battlefields
Testing ideas that fed into the science of flight

packs.name: Sages & Strategists
packs.whatsNew: Five more great thinkers for History ▸ Figures (Mencius, Zhuangzi, Mozi, Sima Qian, Zhuge Liang) plus three key ideas for the Philosophy tab (Ren, Wu Wei, Great Harmony).

history.title: Mencius
history.detailHtml: The most influential Confucian after Confucius himself. Argued that human nature is fundamentally good and that rulers govern only by the people's consent. “The people are the most important element in a nation.”

history.title: Zhuangzi
history.detailHtml: A founding voice of Taoism, famous for playful parables and the “butterfly dream.” Championed spontaneity, relativity of perspective and freedom from convention. “Flow with whatever may happen and let your mind be free.”

history.title: Mozi
history.detailHtml: Founder of Mohism, which preached “universal love” (兼爱), meritocracy and opposition to wasteful warfare. An early logician and engineer whose followers were renowned defensive strategists.

history.title: Sima Qian
history.detailHtml: The “Grand Historian.” His Records of the Grand Historian (史记) set the model for Chinese history-writing for two millennia — completed at enormous personal cost after he chose disgrace over abandoning the work.

history.title: Zhuge Liang
history.detailHtml: Legendary chancellor and strategist of Shu Han during the Three Kingdoms, a byword for wisdom and ingenuity. His Chu Shi Biao memorial is still memorised in schools today.

history.title: Benevolence
history.body: Often translated as humaneness or benevolence — the warm regard for others that, for Confucius, lies at the heart of being fully human. Expressed through empathy, kindness and the reciprocity of “do not impose on others what you would not choose for yourself.”

history.title: Wu Wei
history.body: The Taoist principle of acting in accordance with the natural flow of things — achieving more by forcing less. Not idleness, but action so well-timed and unforced it seems effortless.

history.title: Great Harmony
history.body: A classical vision of a world shared by all, where the worthy are chosen for office, the old and young are cared for, and people work for the common good rather than themselves. A recurring ideal in Chinese political thought.

packs.name: Dynasties & Landmarks
packs.whatsNew: Three more dynasties for the History timeline (Three Kingdoms, Jin, Sui) and four great landmarks (the Terracotta Army, the Grand Canal, the Forbidden City and the Mogao Caves) with their own photos.

history.title: Three Kingdoms
history.summary: Wei, Shu and Wu vie for the empire — the age behind the Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

sections.body: After the Han collapsed, China split into three rival states — Wei (魏), Shu (蜀) and Wu (吴). Decades of war, diplomacy and shifting alliances followed.

list: Immortalised in the classic novel Romance of the Three Kingdoms
Heroes like Zhuge Liang, Cao Cao and Guan Yu became legends
The Battle of Red Cliffs remains one of history's most famous engagements

history.title: Jin Dynasty
history.summary: Briefly reunified China before it fragmented into the Northern and Southern dynasties.

sections.body: The Jin reunited China in 280 CE, ending the Three Kingdoms, but court intrigue and nomadic invasions soon split the realm again, beginning centuries of division.
Despite the turmoil, the era flourished in calligraphy and letters — Wang Xizhi (王羲之), the “Sage of Calligraphy,” lived in this period.

history.title: Sui Dynasty
history.summary: Reunified a divided China and built the Grand Canal — short-lived but foundational.

sections.body: The Sui ended nearly four centuries of division, reunifying north and south and laying the groundwork the Tang would build its golden age upon.

list: Dug the Grand Canal linking north and south
Revived and expanded the imperial examination system
Rebuilt and extended the Great Wall

sections.body: Vast public works and failed military campaigns exhausted the people; revolts ended the dynasty after just two emperors.

history.title: Terracotta Army
history.summary: Thousands of life-sized clay soldiers guarding the First Emperor's tomb.

sections.body: Buried near Qin Shi Huang around 210 BCE and rediscovered by well-diggers in 1974, the Terracotta Army numbers over 8,000 soldiers — each with a unique face — plus horses and chariots.
One of the greatest archaeological finds of the 20th century and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, it reveals the scale and craft of the Qin empire.

history.title: The Grand Canal
history.summary: The world's longest and oldest artificial waterway.

sections.body: The Grand Canal (大运河) is the longest and oldest artificial waterway in the world. Running roughly 1,800 km from Beijing in the north to Hangzhou in the south, it stitched the Chinese heartland together for more than a thousand years.

Though sections dated back centuries, the Sui emperor Yang united them into a single system around 605–610 CE. Millions of labourers were conscripted; the human cost was staggering and helped topple the short-lived Sui — but the canal endured long after the dynasty fell.

list: Linked five of China's great river systems, including the Yellow and Yangtze
Pioneered the pound lock (~10th c.) to raise and lower boats between water levels
Lined with towpaths, granaries, bridges and canal-side towns

sections.body: The canal carried the all-important grain tribute from the rich southern rice lands to feed the northern capitals and armies. It knit a fertile south to a governing north, making a unified empire economically possible.
Named a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2014, large stretches still carry barge traffic and feed the modern South–North Water Transfer Project — a 1,400-year-old artery still beating.

history.title: The Forbidden City
history.summary: The vast imperial palace at the heart of Beijing.

sections.body: Built by the Ming in the early 1400s, the Forbidden City was home to 24 emperors across the Ming and Qing. Its 980 buildings sit within a moat and walls covering 72 hectares.
The world's largest surviving palace complex and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, it now houses the Palace Museum and its imperial treasures.

history.title: Mogao Caves
history.summary: Centuries of Buddhist art carved into the cliffs of Dunhuang.

sections.body: Along the Silk Road near Dunhuang, 492 cave temples hold a thousand years of Buddhist murals and statues — begun in the 4th century and added to for a millennium.
A treasure-house of Silk Road art and manuscripts, including the world's oldest dated printed book, the Diamond Sutra of 868 CE.

packs.name: Great Figures of China
packs.whatsNew: Six more key figures for History ▸ Figures — Emperor Taizong, the Kangxi Emperor, the poets Li Bai and Du Fu, the heroine Hua Mulan and the physician Li Shizhen.

history.title: Emperor Taizong
history.detailHtml: Architect of the Tang golden age. Won power, then ruled with rare humility — welcoming criticism from his ministers. “With bronze as a mirror one can correct one's appearance; with history as a mirror, one can understand the rise and fall of a state.”

history.title: Kangxi Emperor
history.detailHtml: The longest-reigning emperor in Chinese history — 61 years. Consolidated the Qing, patronised the arts and sciences, and commissioned the great Kangxi Dictionary of more than 47,000 characters.

history.title: Li Bai
history.detailHtml: The “Immortal of Poetry” (诗仙). Romantic, wine-loving and free-spirited, he is perhaps the most beloved poet in the Chinese language. “We never grow tired of each other, the mountain and I.”

history.title: Du Fu
history.detailHtml: The “Sage of Poetry” (诗圣). Where Li Bai soared, Du Fu observed — his verse is a compassionate record of ordinary people's suffering during the Tang's decline, prized for its moral seriousness and craft.

history.title: Hua Mulan
history.detailHtml: The legendary heroine who disguised herself as a man to take her aged father's place in the army, serving with distinction for years. A timeless symbol of filial devotion and courage, first told in the Ballad of Mulan.

history.title: Li Shizhen
history.detailHtml: The Ming physician who spent 27 years compiling the Bencao Gangmu (本草纲目) — a monumental pharmacopoeia of nearly 1,900 medicines that influenced medicine and natural science for centuries.

packs.name: The Treasure Ship Era
packs.whatsNew: Dive into the Ming maritime golden age — Admiral Zheng He and the Yongle Emperor, the colossal treasure ships and their watertight hulls, and the famous tribute giraffe hailed as a qilin.

history.title: Zheng He
history.detailHtml: The Ming admiral who led seven epic voyages (1405–1433) with fleets of hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of men, reaching Southeast Asia, India, Arabia and the east coast of Africa — decades before the European age of exploration. A Muslim eunuch who rose to command the greatest fleet the world had yet seen.

history.title: Yongle Emperor
history.detailHtml: The ambitious third Ming emperor who commissioned Zheng He's treasure voyages, moved the capital to Beijing and built the Forbidden City, and ordered the vast Yongle Encyclopedia. His reign projected Chinese power and prestige across the known world.

sections.body: The flagships of Zheng He's fleet were the largest wooden ships of their age — by some accounts over 120 metres long, with up to nine masts and dozens of cabins. Even at a fraction of those figures they dwarfed the European caravels that followed.

list: Multiple masts with battened sails for power and control
Watertight bulkhead compartments below decks
Sternpost rudders to steer the huge hulls

sections.body: Chinese shipwrights divided a hull into sealed bulkhead compartments, so a breach would flood only one section and the ship stayed afloat — a safety idea Western shipbuilding would not adopt for centuries.
It made the treasure ships' enormous size and long ocean crossings possible, and the principle is still used in ships today.

history.title: The Tribute Giraffe
history.summary: An African giraffe presented to the Ming court — hailed as the mythical qilin.

sections.body: In 1414 Zheng He's fleet brought home a giraffe from the coast of East Africa. With its gentle nature and strange form, the court hailed it as the qilin (麒麟) — a mythical beast said to appear only in an age of sage rule.
Such tribute showed the reach of the voyages and flattered the Yongle Emperor as a wise and virtuous ruler whose realm drew wonders from the ends of the earth.

packs.name: Schools of Thought
packs.whatsNew: A rich, illustrated tour of Chinese philosophy for History ▸ Philosophy — Confucianism, Taoism, Legalism, Mohism and Buddhism, plus the idea of Qi, each with its own photo.

history.title: Confucianism
history.detailHtml:
Founded on the teachings of Confucius (孔子, 551–479 BCE), Confucianism holds that a good society grows from good people. It stresses moral self-cultivation, respect within the family, ritual propriety (礼) and benevolence (仁).

For two thousand years it shaped Chinese government, education and family life, and its ideal of the cultivated junzi (君子, “noble person”) remains a cultural touchstone. “Do not impose on others what you do not wish for yourself.”

history.title: Taoism
history.summary: Living in harmony with the Way
history.detailHtml:
Rooted in Laozi's Tao Te Ching (道德经) and the parables of Zhuangzi, Taoism teaches harmony with the Dao (道) — the natural order underlying all things.

Its central ideal, wu wei (无为, effortless action), is doing by not forcing. Taoism nourished Chinese medicine, martial arts, landscape painting and a deep love of nature. “The Way that can be spoken of is not the eternal Way.”

history.title: Legalism
history.detailHtml:
Systematised by Han Feizi (韩非子), Legalism argued that human nature is selfish and that order comes only from clear laws, firm rewards and harsh punishments applied equally to all.

It was the ruthless engine behind the Qin unification of China in 221 BCE — hugely effective, but so severe that the Qin collapsed within a generation, and later dynasties softened it with Confucian ethics.

history.title: Mohism
history.detailHtml:
Founded by Mozi (墨子), Mohism preached universal love (兼爱) — caring for everyone equally — and opposed wasteful luxury and aggressive war.

Unusually for its time, the Mohists prized logic, evidence and engineering, and were famous as skilled builders of defensive fortifications. A major rival to Confucianism in the classical age, before fading under the empire.

history.title: Chinese Buddhism
history.detailHtml:
Arriving from India along the Silk Road around the 1st century CE, Buddhism blended with native thought to become a pillar of Chinese culture.

It teaches that craving causes suffering and that release comes through wisdom and compassion. In China it flowered into distinct traditions — most famously Chan (禅), which travelled on to Japan as Zen.

history.title: Qi
history.detailHtml:
Qi (气) is the vital energy believed to flow through all living things and the cosmos itself. Balanced, free-flowing qi means health and vitality; blocked or depleted qi means illness.

The idea underpins traditional Chinese medicine, acupuncture, feng shui and the slow, breath-led movements of tai chi (太极) and qigong (气功).

packs.name: Chinese Food: Everyday Classics
packs.whatsNew: Unlocks a brand-new Chinese Food section — 8 classic home-style recipes with photos, ingredient lists and step-by-step methods, browsable by ingredient.

recipes.name: Kung Pao Chicken
recipes.description: A Sichuan classic of diced chicken stir-fried with peanuts, dried chilies and a glossy sweet-savoury sauce — quick, punchy and a takeaway favourite the world over.
ingredients: 2 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tbsp black vinegar, 1 tsp sugar
steps: Marinate the diced chicken in 1 tbsp soy sauce and the cornflour slurry for 10 minutes.
Mix the remaining soy sauce, vinegar and sugar into a sauce.
Stir-fry the chilies briefly in hot oil, then add the chicken and cook until golden.
Add garlic, ginger and spring onion, then pour in the sauce and toss until glossy.
Stir through the peanuts and serve with steamed rice.

recipes.name: Mapo Tofu
recipes.description: Silky tofu in a fiery, numbing Sichuan sauce of doubanjiang and peppercorns. Comforting, bold and ready in minutes.
steps: Gently blanch the tofu cubes in salted water, then drain.
Fry the mince until browned, add doubanjiang, garlic and ginger until fragrant and red.
Pour in the stock, slide in the tofu and simmer gently for 4-5 minutes.
Thicken with the cornflour slurry, sprinkle with Sichuan pepper and spring onion.

recipes.name: Sweet & Sour Pork
recipes.description: Crispy battered pork tossed in a bright, tangy sauce with peppers and pineapple — the perfect balance of sweet and sour.
ingredients: 3 tbsp ketchup, 2 tbsp vinegar, 2 tbsp sugar, 1 tbsp soy
steps: Coat the pork in egg then cornflour and deep-fry until golden and crisp; set aside.
Stir together the ketchup, vinegar, sugar and soy into a sauce.
Stir-fry the pepper and onion briefly, add the sauce and pineapple and bring to a glaze.
Toss the fried pork through the sauce just before serving so it stays crisp.

recipes.name: Egg Fried Rice
recipes.description: The ultimate quick meal — day-old rice tossed with egg, peas and spring onion over a high flame. Simple done well.
ingredients: 3 cups cold cooked rice (ideally a day old)
2 tbsp soy sauce, pinch of white pepper
steps: Scramble the eggs in hot oil and set aside.
Add the rice and stir-fry over high heat, breaking up any clumps.
Return the egg, add peas and spring onion, season with soy and pepper.
Toss until everything is hot and the rice is lightly toasted.

recipes.name: Pork Dumplings
recipes.description: Handmade jiaozi are the heart of family gatherings, especially at New Year. Juicy pork and cabbage wrapped and pleated by hand.
ingredients: 2 cups napa cabbage, finely chopped & squeezed
2 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tbsp sesame oil
steps: Mix the pork, cabbage, ginger, spring onion, soy and sesame oil into a filling.
Place a spoonful on each wrapper, wet the edge and pleat closed.
Boil in batches until they float and the filling is cooked, about 6-8 minutes.
Serve with a dip of soy, vinegar and chili oil.

recipes.name: Hot & Sour Soup
recipes.description: A warming, tangy soup of tofu, mushrooms and egg ribbons — sour with vinegar, hot with white pepper.
ingredients: 3 tbsp black vinegar, 1 tsp white pepper, 2 tbsp soy
steps: Bring the stock to a simmer and add the mushrooms and tofu.
Season with soy, vinegar and plenty of white pepper.
Thicken with the cornflour slurry until silky.
Turn off the heat and stir in the beaten egg in a thin stream to form ribbons.

recipes.name: Beef Chow Mein
recipes.description: Springy noodles stir-fried with tender beef and crisp vegetables in a savoury sauce — a wok favourite.
ingredients: 3 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tbsp oyster sauce, 1 tsp sesame oil
steps: Sear the beef in a very hot wok, then remove.
Stir-fry the garlic and vegetables until just tender.
Add the noodles and sauces, tossing to coat.
Return the beef, toss through and finish with sesame oil.

recipes.name: Garlic Bok Choy
recipes.description: A bright, simple side of baby bok choy flashed with garlic — the green that finishes almost every Chinese meal.
steps: Heat oil and fry the garlic until just fragrant (don't brown it).
Add the bok choy and a splash of water, tossing over high heat.
Add the oyster sauce and sugar and stir-fry until the stems are crisp-tender.
Serve immediately while vivid green.

packs.name: Chinese Food: Dumplings & Dim Sum
packs.whatsNew: 8 dumpling and dim sum classics for the Chinese Food section — xiaolongbao, har gow, siu mai, char siu bao, potstickers, spring rolls, wonton soup and egg tarts.

recipes.name: Soup Dumplings
recipes.description: Shanghai's famous xiaolongbao — delicate steamed parcels that burst with hot soup. The trick is folding gelled stock into the filling, which melts as they steam.
ingredients: 1 cup pork-stock aspic (chilled until set), diced
2 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tbsp Shaoxing wine, 1 tsp sugar
Black vinegar & ginger slivers, to serve
steps: Mix the pork, seasonings, ginger and spring onion, then fold through the diced aspic.
Place a spoonful on each wrapper and pleat into a sealed purse with a twisted top.
Steam over high heat for 8 minutes until the filling is cooked and soupy.
Lift carefully, dip in black vinegar with ginger, and bite gently to sip the soup first.

recipes.name: Crystal Shrimp Dumplings
recipes.description: The benchmark of any dim sum kitchen — plump prawns wrapped in a translucent, springy wheat-starch skin pleated into a crescent.
ingredients: 1 tsp sesame oil, pinch of sugar, white pepper
Wheat starch + tapioca starch dough (the clear wrapper)
steps: Mix the prawns with bamboo shoot, sesame oil, sugar and pepper; chill.
Make the wrapper dough with boiling water so it turns translucent, and roll thin discs.
Fill and pleat into the classic crescent with 7-10 folds.
Steam for about 6 minutes until the wrappers turn glassy.

recipes.name: Siu Mai
recipes.description: Open-topped pork and prawn dumplings in a thin yellow wrapper — a dim sum trolley staple, often crowned with a dot of roe.
ingredients: 1 tbsp soy, 1 tsp sesame oil, 1 tsp sugar
steps: Combine pork, prawns, mushroom and seasonings into a sticky filling.
Push a spoonful into a wrapper, squeezing to form an open-topped cup.
Tap flat-bottomed and top with a little roe or diced carrot.
Steam for 8-10 minutes until firm and juicy.

recipes.name: BBQ Pork Buns
recipes.description: Pillowy steamed buns that split open to reveal sweet-savoury barbecue pork — a dim sum favourite for all ages.
ingredients: 2 tbsp oyster sauce, 1 tbsp hoisin, 1 tsp sugar
steps: Cook the sauce ingredients with the pork until thick and glossy; cool.
Roll the proved dough into discs, add filling and pleat closed.
Prove again until puffy, then steam for 12 minutes.
Serve warm — the tops should crack open slightly.

recipes.name: Potstickers
recipes.description: Pan-fried dumplings with a crisp golden base and steamed juicy top — the best of both textures in one bite.
steps: Fill and fold the dumplings, sealing the tops.
Fry flat-side down in oil until the bottoms are golden.
Add water and cover to steam until the tops are cooked and the water evaporates.
Uncover to re-crisp the base, then serve crispy-side up.

recipes.name: Spring Rolls
recipes.description: Shatteringly crisp rolls packed with stir-fried vegetables — eaten at Spring Festival for their resemblance to gold bars.
ingredients: 1 tbsp soy, 1 tsp sesame oil; oil for frying
steps: Stir-fry the filling until just tender, season, and cool completely.
Roll tightly in the wrappers, sealing the edge with a little water.
Deep-fry at 180°C until golden and crisp.
Drain and serve hot with a sweet chili or soy dip.

recipes.name: Wonton Soup
recipes.description: Silky pork-and-prawn wontons floating in a clear, comforting broth — light, slurpable and quick.
ingredients: 1 tsp ginger, 1 tbsp soy, 1 tsp sesame oil
4 cups chicken stock, greens & spring onion
steps: Mix the filling and wrap a small amount in each wonton skin.
Bring the stock to a gentle simmer and season lightly.
Poach the wontons until they float and turn translucent, 3-4 minutes.
Ladle into bowls with the broth and greens.

recipes.name: Egg Tarts
recipes.description: The sweet finale of dim sum — flaky pastry shells filled with a wobbling, glossy egg custard.
ingredients: Shortcrust or puff pastry, in tart tins
1/2 cup sugar dissolved in 1 cup hot water
1/4 cup evaporated milk, splash of vanilla
steps: Line tart tins with pastry and chill.
Whisk the eggs with the cooled sugar-water and milk, then strain for a smooth custard.
Fill the shells two-thirds full.
Bake at 200°C for ~15-18 minutes until just set with a slight wobble.

packs.name: Chinese Food: Sichuan Classics
packs.whatsNew: 8 bold Sichuan classics for the Chinese Food section — dan dan noodles, twice-cooked pork, boiled fish, fish-fragrant eggplant, laziji, hotpot, red oil wontons and smashed cucumber.

recipes.name: Dan Dan Noodles
recipes.description: Chengdu street food at its best — springy noodles under a slick of chili oil, crowned with crisp savoury pork and crushed peanuts. Toss before eating.
ingredients: 2 tbsp preserved mustard greens (ya cai), chopped
2 tbsp chili oil, 1 tbsp soy, 1 tsp Sichuan peppercorn (ground)
1 tbsp sesame paste, crushed peanuts & scallions
steps: Fry the pork with the ya cai until dark, crisp and fragrant.
Spoon chili oil, soy, sesame paste and Sichuan pepper into each bowl.
Boil the noodles and add them with a splash of cooking water.
Top with the pork, peanuts and scallions — toss well before eating.

recipes.name: Twice-Cooked Pork
recipes.description: Pork belly is boiled, then sliced and stir-fried with leeks in spicy bean paste until the edges curl — a true Sichuan home classic.
ingredients: 1 tsp fermented black beans, 1 tsp sweet bean sauce
steps: Boil the pork belly until just cooked, cool, then slice thinly.
Fry the slices in a dry-ish wok until they curl and render fat.
Push aside, fry the doubanjiang and black beans until red and fragrant.
Add the leeks and seasonings, toss briefly and serve.

recipes.name: Boiled Fish in Chili Oil
recipes.description: Tender fish fillets poached in a fiery sea of chili oil, dried chilies and Sichuan peppercorns. Dramatic to serve, surprisingly delicate to eat.
ingredients: 2 tbsp doubanjiang, a big handful dried chilies
Beansprouts or napa cabbage, 3 cups stock
steps: Velvet the fish in egg white and cornflour.
Blanch the beansprouts and lay in a deep bowl.
Fry the doubanjiang in oil, add stock, then gently poach the fish; pour over the sprouts.
Top with chilies and peppercorns, then pour over smoking-hot oil to release the aroma.

recipes.name: Fish-Fragrant Eggplant
recipes.description: No fish involved — "fish-fragrant" is a sweet, sour, spicy, garlicky Sichuan flavour. Silky eggplant soaks it all up.
ingredients: 1.5 tbsp doubanjiang, 3 cloves garlic + 1 tsp ginger
1 tbsp soy, 1 tbsp sugar, 1 tbsp black vinegar
steps: Fry or roast the eggplant until soft and golden.
Fry the doubanjiang, garlic and ginger until red and fragrant.
Add soy, sugar and vinegar, return the eggplant and toss to coat.
Thicken with the slurry and finish with spring onion.

recipes.name: Chongqing Chicken
recipes.description: A mountain of dried chilies hiding little nuggets of crunchy fried chicken — the fun is hunting for them. Numbing, smoky and addictive.
ingredients: Marinade: 1 tbsp soy, 1 tbsp wine, cornflour
steps: Marinate the chicken, then deep-fry until deeply golden and crisp.
In a little oil, toast the chilies, peppercorns, garlic and ginger until fragrant (don't burn).
Return the chicken and toss to coat in the aromatics.
Serve dry — pick the crisp chicken from the chilies.

recipes.name: Sichuan Hotpot
recipes.description: Less a dish than an event — a bubbling cauldron of chili-and-peppercorn broth at the table, into which everyone dips meat, tofu and vegetables.
ingredients: Hotpot base (doubanjiang, chilies, Sichuan peppercorn, aromatics)
Dipping sauce: sesame oil, garlic, coriander
steps: Fry the hotpot base, then add stock and simmer to build a deep, spicy broth.
Bring the pot to the table on a burner, keeping it at a gentle boil.
Dip raw ingredients in to cook to your liking — thin meats take seconds.
Fish them out into your sesame-garlic dipping bowl.

recipes.name: Red Oil Wontons
recipes.description: Plump pork wontons bathed in a sweet, garlicky chili oil rather than soup — Sichuan's punchy answer to the wonton.
ingredients: Pork wontons (200g pork, ginger, soy, sesame oil; wrappers)
1 tbsp soy, 1 tsp black vinegar, 1 tsp sugar
steps: Wrap and boil the wontons until they float.
Whisk the chili oil, soy, vinegar, sugar and garlic into a sauce in a bowl.
Drain the wontons and toss through the red oil sauce.
Scatter with scallions and serve warm.

recipes.name: Smashed Cucumber
recipes.description: The cooling counterpoint to all that chili — cucumber smashed so it drinks up a garlicky, tangy dressing. Five minutes of work.
ingredients: 1 tbsp soy, 1 tbsp black vinegar, 1 tsp sugar
1 tbsp chili oil, 1 tsp sesame oil, pinch of salt
steps: Smash the cucumbers with the flat of a knife, then tear into bite-size pieces.
Salt briefly and drain off the excess water.
Whisk the garlic, soy, vinegar, sugar and oils into a dressing.
Toss just before serving so it stays crunchy.
